coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of triclosan by Lanthanide-Functionalized Metal–Organic frameworks
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Qiguo
Chen, Ting
Zhang, Min
Chen, Xiaofeng
coredata.dc:description: As a widely used antibacterial and antifungal agent in numerous personal care and consumer products, triclosan (TCS) is frequently detected in natural water bodies and has caused serious harm on human health and ecosystems. The quantitative and fast analysis of TCS is thus of paramount significance, however still remains challenging. In recent years, ratiometric fluorescence sensing has attracted vast interests in analyte detection due to the dual emission, which can effectively eliminate interference from environmental factors and improve the detection accuracy. Benefiting from the strong encapsulation capability and structural adjustability, metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are demonstrated to be a perfect platform to construct ratiometric fluorescence sensors. In the present study, we modify a zirconium MOF, UiO-67-bpy, to be a ratiometric fluorescence sensor by introducing Eu3+ and carbon dots sequentially via the post-synthetic method. The obtained hybrid system, CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy, shows two well separated emission peaks. With the addition of TCS, the red fluorescence from Eu3+ at 611 nm decreases while the blue fluorescence from carbon dots at 412 nm remains unchanged, resulting an excellent ratiometric response. Linear relationships of the fluorescence ratio (F412/F611) against the concentration of TCS are acquired in both low and high concentration range with detection of limit as low as 0.53 and 2.3 μM, respectively. Moreover, satisfactory recoveries of TCS levels from CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy are achieved in various water samples and personal care products. The composite of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy also displays a fluorescence color changing from red to blue responsive to different concentration of TCS, demonstrating great potential in practical application of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy for visualized monitoring TCS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triclosan
Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs)
Post-synthetic modification
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometrci detection of TCS by CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy
Detection performance of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy towards TCS in real water samples
Determination of TCS in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A) and X-ray energy dispersion spectroscopy (EDS) (B) of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy.
PXRD patterns (A), N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (B), pore size distributions (C), and FT-IR (D) of UiO-67-bpy, Eu/UiO-67-bpy, and CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy.

UV–vis absorption (A), fluorescence emission spectra (B) of CDs, H2bpy, Eu/UiO-67-bpy, and CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy under 323 nm excitation. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy before and after the addition of TCS, the inset photo shows the corresponding emission changes under irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A) and CIE chromaticity diagrams (B) of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy after the addition of different concentration of TCS. The fitting curves between the fluorescence ratio (F412/F611) and the low (C) and high (D) concentration of TCS.
Selectivity and anti-interference of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy towards common ions (A) and the structure analogues of TCS (B).

(A) PXRD patterns of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy before and after the addition of TCS. (B) Absorption spectra of TCS and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy. (C) Absorption spectra of TCS and CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy before and after the addition of TCS. (D) Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy before and after addition of TCS.
Schematic illustration of the formation process of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy by post-synthetic modification and the sensing strategy for the ratiometric detection of TCS.
Result of TCS in actual water samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of triclosan by Lanthanide-Functionalized Metal–Organic frameworks

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Geographical Sciences, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai 200234, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Geographical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Geographical Sciences, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai 200234, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a widely used antibacterial and antifungal agent in numerous personal care and consumer products, triclosan (TCS) is frequently detected in natural water bodies and has caused serious harm on human health and ecosystems. The quantitative and fast analysis of TCS is thus of paramount significance, however still remains challenging. In recent years, ratiometric fluorescence sensing has attracted vast interests in analyte detection due to the dual emission, which can effectively eliminate interference from environmental factors and improve the detection accuracy. Benefiting from the strong encapsulation capability and structural adjustability, metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are demonstrated to be a perfect platform to construct ratiometric fluorescence sensors. In the present study, we modify a zirconium MOF, UiO-67-bpy, to be a ratiometric fluorescence sensor by introducing Eu3+ and carbon dots sequentially via the post-synthetic method. The obtained hybrid system, CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy, shows two well separated emission peaks. With the addition of TCS, the red fluorescence from Eu3+ at 611 nm decreases while the blue fluorescence from carbon dots at 412 nm remains unchanged, resulting an excellent ratiometric response. Linear relationships of the fluorescence ratio (F412/F611) against the concentration of TCS are acquired in both low and high concentration range with detection of limit as low as 0.53 and 2.3 μM, respectively. Moreover, satisfactory recoveries of TCS levels from CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy are achieved in various water samples and personal care products. The composite of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy also displays a fluorescence color changing from red to blue responsive to different concentration of TCS, demonstrating great potential in practical application of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy for visualized monitoring TCS.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a broad spectrum antibacterial and antifungal agent, triclosan (TCS) was first developed in the 1960 s, and has been rapidly utilized in a variety of consumer products such as soaps, toothpastes, detergents, and shampoos. [1–3] Currently TCS has a global production volume of over 10 million pounds, [4] and this number likely has increased dramatically due to the high market demand for disinfection since the outbreak of coronavirus pandemic in 2019. Due to its lipophilic properties, TCS is readily absorbed into human skin and oral mucosa via consumer products that contain TCS, and water and/or food products contaminated with TCS. [5,6] The measurable levels of TCS in human and environment systems have arisen concerns for both human health and ecological balance. Studies have reported TCS as an endocrine disrupting chemical, meanwhile TCS might also affect immune responses and cardiovascular function. [2,7,8] Moreover, the growing bacterial resistance and toxicity of TCS on beneficial organisms have also been found to severely interfere the ecosystem. [1,9] Given these high safety risks, the development of analytical methods for monitoring TCS with high sensitivity and fast response is of an increasing need.

Many traditional instrumental techniques have been explored for the determination of TCS, including those based on chromatography-mass spectrometry, electrochemistry, and capillary zone electrophoresis. [10–13] However, these methods generally rely on costly instruments, specialized training, and complex sample preprocessing, which tremendously hinder the practical application. [14] Fluorescence sensing utilizing the change of fluorescence responsive to the analyte, has attracted a great deal of research interests and been widely explored for the detection of various analytes, due to its many beneficial advantages such as high sensitivity, fast response, simplicity of sample preparation, and low cost. [15–19] Despite these attractive characteristics, fluorescence sensing is rarely employed for the detection of TCS and there are only few reported literatures. [12,20–22] Bhogal and colleagues constructed a molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor (CDs@m-MIP) with bule emission, and upon addition of TCS, electrons in the conduction band of the CDs are transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the TCS, resulting in quenching of the fluorescence of the CDs. [10] Based on the phenomenon that contamination of cells with TCS will lead to the loss of mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), Geng and his team designed and synthesized a strongly positively charged and MMP-sensitive carbon nanoprobe (CNP). The obtained MMP-sensitive CNP can be electrostatically attracted and captured in mitochondria through negative transmembrane potential, thus indirectly realizing the fluorescence detection of TCS. [23] Despite the great advantages, fluorescence sensing of TCS still remain greatly unexplored. In contrast to single emission fluorescence sensing, ratiometric fluorescence sensing is a technique based on the ratio of dual emission intensities, which greatly decreases the environmental interference and improves detection accuracy. Many ratiometric fluorescence systems have been constructed for sensing analytes, including ions, [24–26] small molecules, [25,27,28] biomolecules, [29–31] pH, [20,32,33] and temperature [34,35] etc. Due to the structural adjustability, high porosity, and encapsulation capabilities of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), ratiometric MOFs sensors can be easily constructed by utilizing the intrinsic emission from the metal node and/or organic linker, and/or introducing external chromophores. [20,36–38] Therefore, MOFs based ratiometric sensors have received increasing interests with the fast development of novel luminescent MOFs. [32,39–41].

In this work, lanthanide Eu and carbon dots (CDs) are sequentially introduced to a zirconium MOF UiO-67-bpy via post-synthetic method (Scheme1 ). The obtained composite CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy shows two well separated emission peaks at 412 nm and 611 nm respectively, endowing the obtained sensor with excellent visualized fluorescence properties for sensing. The emission intensity at 611 nm decreases with the addition of TCS, while the one at 411 nm remains unchanged, resulting in an intense ratiometric response towards TCS. The practical application of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy also shows excellent recoveries of TCS levels in real water samples and personal care products.

All chemicals are of analytical reagent grade and used as received without further purification. ZrCl4 was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. EuCl3·6H2O and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Energy Chemical. 2, 2′-bipyridine-5, 5′-dicarboxylic acid (H2bpy) was purchased from Shanghai Haohong Biomedicine Technology CO., Ltd. Benzoic acid (BA), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), salicylic acid, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), triclosan (TCS), NaOH, sulfamethoxazole (SMX), enrofloxacin (ENR), tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA), p-Chlorophenol (PCP), 3-Chlorophenol (3-CP), 2, 4-Dichlorophenol (2, 4-DCP), 2, 6-Dichlorophenol (2, 6-DCP), 2, 4, 5-Trichlorophenol (2, 4, 5-TCP), and 2,4,6-Trichlorophenol (2, 4, 6-TCP) were purchased from Adamas. HCl, KI, KCl, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, CdCl2, HgCl2, NaNO3, NaNO2, Na2C2O4, and ciprofloxacin (CIP) were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Millipore Milli-Q ultrapure water was used for the experiments involved.

X-ray diffraction measurements were conducted with a Rigaku D/max-2000 X-ray diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5418 Å). Specific surface area was calculated via the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Pore volume (VP) and pore diameter (DP) were calculated via Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model (NOVOE 4000, Quantachrome). The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu). The emission spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4, HORIBA Scientific). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using an emission scanning electron microscope (S 4800, Hitachi). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements were carried out with a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific). High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was carried out using an UltiMate 3000 pump (Thermo Scientific). All separations were conducted on a Thermo Scientific ZORBAX SB-C18 (250 mm × 4.6 mm, Agilent) column. Methanol and 0.2 % water solution (90/10 vol ratio) comprised the mobile phase. The detection wavelength was 281 nm, the flow rate was 1.0 mL min−1, and the column temperature was set at 35 ℃, injection volume 10 μL. The sample was filtered through a membrane filter before testing. All optical measurements were carried out at ambient temperature.

UiO-67-bpy was synthesized via a solvent thermal method according to the reported literature [42] with slight modifications. Briefly, ZrCl4 (134.0 mg) was dissolved in DMF (30 mL) followed by adding BA (780.0 mg). The obtained mixture was stirred for 15 min and subsequently sonicated for 10 min, then HCl (0.5 mL) and H2bpy (180.0 mg) were added to the system, followed by another 10 min stirring and subsequent 10 min sonication. The obtained homogeneous mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained in an oven at 120 °C for 24 h. The autoclave was then cooled down to room temperature. The product was separated through centrifugation, washed with DMF and MeOH several times, and dried in vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h to provide light gray solid.

The post-modification method was used to introduce Eu3+ into the MOFs system. UiO-67-bpy (100.0 mg) and EuCl3·6H2O (98.0 mg) were introduced to a 15 mL of MeOH solvent. The mixture was sonicated for 15 min, then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor, and placed in an oven at 80 ℃ for 24 h. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature, and the product was collected and washed three times with MeOH. The obtained product was then dried in vacuum oven at 80 ℃ for 12 h to obtain light gray solid (Eu/UiO-67-bpy).

CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using salicylic acid as a carbon source [43]. First, 0.2 g of salicylic acid and 1.0 mL of EDA were dissolved in a 10 mL of EtOH. The solution was then stirred and ultrasonicated for 10 min to get a homogeneous dispersion. The obtained mixture was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained in an oven at 180 °C for 3 h. The autoclave was then cooled down to room temperature, and light yellow liquid was obtained.

Eu/UiO-67-bpy (1 mg) was dispersed into 25 mL EtOH. 1, 2, 3, and 4 mL of CDs were added to the above solutions, respectively. The system was sonicated to obtain a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, the obtained mixture was transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor and placed in an oven at 80 ℃ for 3 h. The reactor was cooled down to room temperature, and the product was collected and washed with EtOH three times. The product was then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ℃ overnight to give the final product CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy.

CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy (2.5 mg) was dispersed in 50 mL of MeOH and water (1:3, v/v), and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min to obtain the homogeneous dispersion of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy at a concentration of 0.05 mg L-1. The dispersion was stored in a refrigerator at 2 ℃. The emission spectra were collected one time per day within 30 days to test the fluorescence stability of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy. CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy (2 mg) were dispersed in 10 mL of a mixture of ultrapure water and MeOH at different pH from 3 to 9 (pH was adjusted by NaOH solution and dilute hydrochloric acid solution). The mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min to make the system uniformly dispersed, and the emission spectra at different pH were collected to test the pH stability of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometrci detection of TCS by CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy in the mixed solvent of MeOH and deionized water (1:3, v/v) was made by sonication for 10 min to give the concentration as 0.05 mg mL−1. A standard solution of TCS was prepared by dissolving 100.0 mg of TCS in 1.0 L of MeOH and water (1:3, v/v). Then the standard solution of TCS (100 mg L-1) was introduced gradually to 2 mL of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy solution (TCS was formulated to a concentration of 10 μM). After incubation for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra were collected under the excitation wavelength of 323 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy towards TCS in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water, river water, and commercially available personal care products including toothpaste and liquid soap were selected to evaluate the detection performance of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy towards TCS for practical application. Tap water sample was directly taken form the tap in the lab. Surface river water sample was collected from local Caohejing River (Shanghai, China), and filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove impurities before subsequent experiments. The commercial personal care products were purchased from local supermarkets in Shanghai, China. The leaching solution samples were prepared by dissolving 1.0 g of product in 10 mL of MeOH under stirring for 5 min and a further sonication for 30 min. The supernatant was collected through centrifugation and then filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane. All the water samples were monitored by HPLC to exclude the presence of TCS. Spiked samples were obtained by adding TCS standard solution to the above pretreated real water samples to obtain a TCS concentration of 10 μM in the spiked samples. Then different volumes of 10 μM TCS stock solution was added to 3 mL of pretreated real water samples containing 0.15 mg of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy to give various concentrations of TCS (1 μM, 3 μM, 5 μM). After incubation for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra was collected under the excitation wavelength of 323 nm. The concentration of TCS in these real water samples was calculated according to the obtained linear equation between TCS concentration and fluorescence ratio. The results were further compared with those from HPLC measurements.

The structural properties of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy are first examined. CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy displays similar octahedron-like structure to that of UiO-67-bpy (Fig. 1 A, S1A), demonstrating the encapsulation of guest molecules does not affect the MOFs structure. EDS mapping further confirms the uniform distribution of Eu element in CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy (Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the PXRD pattern of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy is consistent with the experimental UiO-67-bpy and the simulated one, demonstrating the structure integrity of UiO-67-bpy is well maintained after the incorporation of Eu and CDs. The BET surface area of UiO-67-bpy decreases from 1300 m2·g−1 to 720 m2·g−1 of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy due to the guest molecule incorporation (Fig. 2B and Table S1). [44] However, the pore diameter of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy is quite close to that of UiO-67-bpy, indicating the structural porosity for analyte transportation (Fig. 2C and Table S1). In addition, FTIR spectra of UiO-67-bpy shows the characteristic peaks from the pyridine ring on the linker (Fig. 2D), with C=N stretching vibration at 1541 cm−1 and C-H bending vibration at 865 cm−1. [42,43] Interestingly, these two peaks of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy are both shifted to higher wave numbers, indicating a strong interaction exists between Eu3+ and N on the pyridine units of the linker. XPS spectra show CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy displays the peak from Eu 3d compared to UiO-67-bpy (Fig. S2A), which also demonstrates the successful introduction of Eu to the MOF composite. Two peaks were found for N1s, which can be assigned to the C-N=C bond from the ligand. [44] Both peaks show positive shifts after Eu was introduced, implying a coordination interaction between the pyridine N atom in the ligand and Eu (Fig. S2B). [45–47] The electron binding energies of Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 are not changed, further confirming the interaction happened between Eu and the pyridine N atom in the ligand, not Zr node (Fig. S2C). [48–51].

We further investigate the optical properties of the obtained materials. Eu/UiO-67-bpy displays a strong absorption peak at 295 nm, which can be assigned to π-π* transitions from H2bpydc linker (Fig. 3 A). An additional small shoulder peak from CDs is found at 340 nm in CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy, also demonstrating a successful incorporation of CDs. As shown in Fig. 3B, both the ligand and UiO-67-bpy show no emission, while Eu/UiO-67-bpy exhibits the characteristic emission of Eu at 611 nm through antenna effect. After the introduction of CDs, CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy shows two well separated peaks, which are beneficial to ratiometric sensing by avoiding mutual interference between two signals. The one at 412 nm is the characteristic emission of CDs, while the emission at 611 nm is from the sensitized Eu3+. [52–58] The sensing performance of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy for triclosan (TCS) is preliminarily evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3C, the emission intensity at 611 nm dramatically decreases with the addition of TCS, while that at 412 nm remains unchanged. This ratiometric response demonstrates a great potential of the obtained sensor in detecting TCS. Moreover, the emission color changes greatly from red to blue after TCS was added, providing a visualized analysis by naked eyes (Fig. 3C, inset). The stability tests show that the fluorescence intensity ratio F412/F611 of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy is subtly varied in water within 15 days (Fig. S1B), and remains almost unchanged in the pH range from 3.0 to 9.0 (Fig. S1C). This excellent fluorescent stability of the obtained sensor indicates great potentials of practical application in aqueous environment.

In order to acquire better detection performances of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy towards TCS, both the composition of the MOFs and the detection conditions are optimized. With the increasing content of CDs in CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy, the emission intensity at 412 nm gradually increases (Fig. S3A) while the quenching efficiency towards TCS first increases and then decreases (Fig. S3B). Thus, we select the optimal sample of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy with 2 mL of CDs are used. The effect of solvent on the quenching behavior of TCS is explored. The emission intensity of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy at 412 nm decreases the most when dispersed in water with the addition of trace TCS (Fig. S4A). However, considering the solubility of TCS is quite limited, a mixture of water and MeOH is further chosen as the dispersing solvent. We obtain the highest quenching efficiency while excessive TCS still doesn’t precipitate when the volume ratio of water and MeOH is 3:1(Fig. S4B). The effect of the concentration of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy on sensing performance is also investigated. With the increasing of the sensor concentration, the quenching efficiency first increases slightly, then drops greatly when the concentration is over 0.05 mg mL−1 (Fig. S5). We also explore the incubation time of the detection system. CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy shows fast emission response to TCS within 1 min (Fig. S6), and the quenching efficiency remains relatively stable after 5 min. Based on these experiments, we choose the mixture of MeOH and water with the volume ration of 1:3 as the dispersing solvent, the sensor concentration of 0.05 mg mL−1, and the incubation time of 5 min as the optimal detection conditions for the subsequent experiments.

The sensing performance of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy towards TCS is examined in detail under the optimal detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity at 412 nm remains unaltered with the addition of TCS, while the one at 611 nm decreases gradually. With this ratiometric response, linear relationships are found between the fluorescence intensity ratio F412/F611 and the concentration of TCS within a wide range (Fig. 4C and D). In the low concentration range of TCS (0 ∼ 23.56 μM), the linear equation is fitted as Y=0.000644X+0.27012 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.99177. And a linear equation of Y=0.02832X-0.17731 with R2 of 0.99739 is obtained when the concentration of TCS is in the high concentration range of 23.56 ∼ 61.73 μM. According to International Union of pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.53 μM and 2.30 μM for the low and high concentration range of TCS, respectively. The obtained LODs are comparable with the other reported TCS fluorescence sensing systems, [10,59,60] illustrating an excellent performance of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy for quantitative analysis of TCS. Moreover, the Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram shows the chromaticity moves from red to blue region upon the addition of TCS (Fig. 4B). The corresponding fluorescence photos of the system upon different concentrations of TCS also display bright color changes (Fig. 4B inset), confirming the visualized analysis of various TCS concentration by CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy through naked eyes.

We then evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference performances of the obtained sensor towards TCS detection for further practical applications in complicated real samples. First, common cations (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, NH4+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+), and anions (Cl-, NO2 –, NO3 –, I-, SO4 2-) in aqueous environment are selected. As shown in Fig. 5 A, these ions do not cause any enhancement of the fluorescence intensity ratio F412/F611 of the sensing system CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy, while obvious increasing of F412/F611 is observed after the addition of TCS. This phenomena illustrates excellent selectivity and anti-interference of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy in sensing TCS among these common ions. We further examine the ability of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy to discriminate TCS from its structure analogues, such as MSX, TBBPA, ENR, PCP, 3-CP, 2, 4-DCP, 2, 6-DCP, 2, 4, 5-DCP, 2, 4, 6-DCP (structures can be found in Fig. S7). CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy doesn’t show any obvious fluorescence response to these structure analogues, while the subsequent addition of TCS to the system leads to remarkable enhancement of F412/F611 (Fig. 5B). These results demonstrate CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy exhibits great anti-interference properties and can selectively detect TCS in real samples.

The mechanism of this ratiometric sensing towards TCS is further explored. We first examine the structure integrity of MOFs.The PXRD patterns of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy remain unchanged after the addition of TCS (Fig. 6 A), thus ruling out the possibility of its structure transformation that might cause the fluorescence emission change. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE) are the three mainly reported mechanisms for fluorescence quenching. [61–64] We collect the absorption spectra of TCS and the emission of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy, no overlap is found between two spectra (Fig. 6B), demonstrating FRET doesn’t exist in the system. However, the absorption spectra of CDs@ Eu/UiO-67-bpy is well overlapped with that of TCS, thus, IFE is regarded to account for the fluorescence quenching of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy (Fig. 6C). The lifetime of CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy decreases from 6.92 ns to 2.64 ns (Fig. 6D), demonstrating a dynamic quenching process between TCS and CDs@Eu/UiO-67-bpy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TCS in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Given the arisen concerns of TCS on both human health and the ecosystem, the evaluation of its existence in practical samples is vital for the subsequent treatment. Here, we also test the detection performance of our ratiometric sensor CDs@ Eu/UiO-67-bpy towards TCS in four common water samples (tap water, river water, commercially available personal care products including toothpaste and liquid soap) where TCS can be easily found. The existence of FLO in these water samples is first excluded by HPLC technique, and TCS is then spiked into the samples. As illustrated in Table 1 , we successfully acquire satisfactory recoveries with low RSDs for all the real water samples. The concentrations of TCS measured by CDs@ Eu/UiO-67-bpy are also relatively consistent with those from HPLC, further demonstrating the feasibility of the obtained sensor for detecting TCS in practical samples.

In summary, Eu3+ and CDs are successfully introduced into UiO-67-bpy by hydrothermal synthesis and post-synthesis modification, and a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe, CDs@ Eu/UiO-67-bpy, is obtained for ratiometric fluorescence detection of the novel pollutant TCS. The presence of TCS quenches the fluorescence form Eu3+ in CDs@ Eu/UiO-67-bpy at 611 nm, whereas the fluorescence intensity of CDs, which is an internal standard, remains unchanged at 412 nm. The ratiometric fluorescent probe showed good linearity in both the low (0–22.56 μM) and high (22.56–60.73 μM) concentration ranges, with excellent selectivity and immunity to interferences, and the detection limits were 0.53 and 2.30 μM, respectively. Practical application of the ratiometric fluorescent probe shows that CDs@ Eu/UiO-67-bpy has great potential for the detection of CDs@ Eu/UiO-67-bpy in tap and river water as well as in the leaching solution of personal care products.

Qiguo Zhong: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ting Chen: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Min Zhang: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Xiaofeng Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21803038). We thank Dr. Yongming Zhang and Dr. Caizhi Liao for their helpful discussions.
